comment.body:
vanna is the vol vs delta for dealers, or vega vs spot.
vanna flip is the zero vanna line, where vanna goes  from negative to positive and vice versa.
it is simple: negative menas inverse relation ship, positive means linear/direct relationship between the two dataset:

negative vanna = vol up/delta down ---> putting MMs into shorter delta, so they buy futs
vol down/delta up ---> putting dealers into longer delta, so they sell futs

positive vanna = vol up/delta up; vol down/delta down...

also

negative vanna = spot up/vega down ---> dealers buy vega (only longer tenors)
spot down/vega up ---> dealers sell vega

positive vanna = spot up/vega up, spot down/vega down

Subject: Oil Market Situation – Strategic Update

Good morning,

After averaging the price, the situation turned in our favor thanks to the escalation of the Israel-Iran conflict. However, the question remains: how long can this last? Just yesterday afternoon, on rumors that the Iranian government might request a safe passage to Russia, oil lost about 3%, later recovering without surpassing Friday’s highs.

It’s important to note that Trump is actively working on several fronts to bring this situation to an end. He’s deploying some of his most influential allies, as he wants oil prices to remain low to avoid further inflation—pressuring Powell to reduce interest rates and ease the federal deficit.

Moreover, how long can Iran continue launching missiles toward Israel, considering they’ve already supplied drones to Putin?

Given all this, I’d prefer to stay on the sidelines for now, ready to shift our positions to the short side—locking in the recovery achieved so far and potentially riding a downward move in oil prices with the goal of fully recovering our capital.

Looking forward to your thoughts.

Thank you,

comment.body:
So because financial markets don’t operate in a normal distribution, you have to account for that discrepancy using something other than a standard geometric brownian motion model (Gaussian distribution). t-distribution is much more similar to modeling markets because the tail ends of the curve (outliers in price movements) are typically higher. Not only that but volatility tends to cluster, and so you have to account for that fact as well (hence the HMM model). 

The HMM I’m building is integrated with my  GARCH model and OU process, but the problem I’m having is the python for HMM’s is simple and can only use Gaussian distribution, but I can create a mixed model with fatter tails to be a better model for financial markets. 

Pomegranate library from MIT is much more flexible and I could directly code in t-distribution but for some reason I cannot seem to import and run field from anaconda through python (I think anaconda was destined for Linux and that’s why). Just trying to see the best approach to this problem

comment.body:
My understanding:
When vol of vol is elevated, the short vanna effect tends to be significant—especially for calls above spot. This implies that dealers are holding a large number of long VIX futures, beta-hedged with short ES positions.

Assuming vol of vol begins to mean-revert, the unwinding of these hedges would involve selling VIX futures and buying ES, which creates supportive flow for the market.
